APPROPRIATENESS.justification: This paper studies word embedding design choices (context types and representations) across standard embedding methods (generalized Skip‑Gram/CBOW and GloVe) and evaluates them on canonical intrinsic (WordSim353, Google analogies) and extrinsic (CoNLL POS/chunking/NER) benchmarks, which squarely fits top-tier NLP/ML conference topics. The methodology, datasets, and metrics are those expected by the community, and the addition of an implementation (word2vecPM) increases practical relevance. The problem formulation and positioning—systematic empirical comparison of context choices for embeddings—address the right audience and standards for such venues.

CLARITY.justification: Based on the provided summary, the paper appears well-organized (clear progression from methods to experiments and conclusions) and states its main contributions early (systematic comparison of context types/representations across embedding methods). Notation and experimental details seem consistently reported (hyperparameters and datasets specified), and the writing quality is described as good. The summary does not comment on the quality of figures/tables or give full certainty about notation detail, so I refrain from a top score.

IMPACT.justification: The paper tackles an important, practical question in embedding design by systematically comparing context types (linear vs dependency) and context representations (bound vs unbound) across multiple embedding algorithms (generalized Skip-Gram/CBOW and GloVe) on both intrinsic (WordSim353, Google analogies) and extrinsic (POS, chunking, NER) tasks. Its key finding—that context representation can have a larger effect than context type and that dependency-based contexts require bound representations to capture functional similarity—would likely change how researchers and practitioners choose/contextualize context signals when training embeddings. By evaluating multiple models, reporting hyperparameters, showing downstream gains, and releasing an implementation (word2vecPM), the work is positioned to be widely used and to spur follow-up work on context representations and cross-lingual extensions, though its broader implications outside NLP are limited.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares against appropriate and standard baselines (Skip-Gram/CBOW variants, GloVe, and original word2vec) and evaluates all methods on the same training corpus (Wikipedia dump) and the same intrinsic and downstream metrics. It explicitly reports when prior approaches perform comparably or worse (e.g., intrinsic results not translating to downstream tasks; dependency contexts needing bound representations; GloVe’s sensitivity to context representation) and systematically analyzes how context type vs representation affects each embedding method. Related work coverage is described as adequate and relevant competing approaches (e.g., Vulic & Korhonen 2016) are discussed.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper’s main claims are clearly articulated and appear supported by extensive experiments across appropriate datasets (Wikipedia training corpus; WordSim353; Google analogies; CoNLL POS/Chunking/NER) using multiple embedding methods (generalized Skip‑Gram/CBOW and GloVe) implemented in word2vecPM. There are no formal mathematical proofs to verify, and the reported hyperparameter/control choices and comparisons to baselines (word2vec/GloVe) indicate a sound experimental methodology; however the summary does not mention statistical significance testing, which is a minor methodological omission. The specific findings (dependency contexts needing bound representations, linear contexts suiting topical similarity, and GloVe being more sensitive to context representation) are consistent with the described experiments, so the conclusions are largely justified by the results.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper performs a systematic empirical comparison of context types (linear vs dependency) and representations (bound vs unbound) across multiple established embedding methods (generalized Skip-Gram/CBOW and GloVe) and introduces an implementation (word2vecPM) to train these variants. These are useful and non-trivial engineering and analysis contributions, but they build on well-known methods (word2vec, GloVe, dependency contexts) rather than proposing a fundamentally new algorithm or theoretical perspective. The work therefore represents an incremental but informative advance that would interest practitioners and researchers, rather than a highly novel breakthrough.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper runs a broad set of experiments comparing context types (linear windows k=2,5 and dependency-based), context representations (bound vs unbound), and three embedding algorithms (Skip-Gram/CBOW variants and GloVe) and evaluates on multiple intrinsic (WordSim, Google analogies) and extrinsic (POS, chunking, NER) tasks, with hyperparameters reported—supporting the main claims and providing useful ablations (e.g., sensitivity of GloVe and the impact of representation vs type). Related work coverage is reported as adequate and limitations (e.g., intrinsic vs downstream mismatch, dependency contexts not sufficient alone, possible language-transfer limits) are discussed. Remaining gaps include lack of evidence beyond English/Wikipedia and some ambiguity about code availability for full reproducibility, so the study is strong but not exhaustive.

main_claims: Systematically compare linear (bag-of-words) vs dependency-based contexts and bound vs unbound context representations across three embedding methods (generalized Skip-Gram/CSG, generalized CBOW/GBOW, and GloVe), showing context representation often has larger impact than context type.

Dependency-based contexts better capture functional similarity, but this benefit largely depends on using a bound context representation; linear contexts better capture topical/relatedness similarity with unbound representations.
Context representation choice can have a larger effect than context type on word analogy performance, and GloVe is more sensitive to context representation than Skip-Gram/CBOW.

For sequence-labeling downstream tasks (POS, chunking, NER), bound representations and dependency-based contexts generally yield better performance; the authors provide an implementation (word2vecPM) to train generalized-context embeddings.
_summary.methodology: Modify and extend word2vec (word2vecPM) to train generalized Skip-Gram, CBOW, and GloVe using different context types (linear window k=2 or k=5 and dependency-based collapsed relations) and two context representations (bound vs unbound), then evaluate embeddings on intrinsic (WordSim353, Google analogy) and extrinsic (POS, chunking, NER) tasks.
datasets: Google analogy dataset (semantic and syntactic partitions)
limitations: Intrinsic evaluation results do not necessarily translate to downstream task performance (intrinsic tasks aren’t necessarily correlated with downstream tasks).
Dependency-based context alone is not sufficient to capture functional similarity; it requires bound representation to be effective.
GloVe is more sensitive to context representation choices than Skip-Gram and CBOW.
Advantages of dependency-based contexts observed for English may not transfer as well to other languages (citing Vulic & Korhonen, 2016).
